story.headline: US bombs Iran for second straight day as Hormuz standoff escalates and oil jumps
story.summary: US struck dozens of targets in Iran Monday using attack sea drones for the first time; Iran fired on Gulf bases and says Hormuz is closed while the US insists it is escorting traffic; Brent rose ~2.8%.
story.display_body: US Central Command said the latest raids hit multiple sites and, for the first time in the campaign, used one-way attack sea drones; Iran said it struck American military bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, Oman and Qatar in retaliation. Washington and Tehran gave conflicting accounts of the waterway itself — Iran insists the strait (which normally carries about 20% of the world's seaborne oil) is closed and has warned vessels away, while US officials say it remains open and are actively escorting commercial traffic through it. Brent crude for September delivery rose about 2.8% on Monday morning to roughly $78 a barrel. Iran said the strikes had "reduced to nothing" the interim peace deal reached last month after 60 days of talks, which had been meant to reopen Hormuz and end the war that began on 28 February.
story.why: The fighting has settled into a dangerous rhythm centred on the world's most important oil chokepoint, with each round nudging energy prices up and burying the diplomacy Oman and Qatar had brokered.

story.headline: Coalition of the willing meets in Paris to boost Ukraine support
story.summary: Macron hosted 25+ leaders of a 37-nation coalition at the Invalides on 13 July, discussing €70bn 2026 aid, Patriot production and a possible 15,000-strong reassurance force.
story.display_body: More than 25 heads of state and government attended, from a coalition — created by France and Britain — that has now grown to 37 states, with Moldova and North Macedonia joining the consultations for the first time; Volodymyr Zelensky was present and is expected to stay for the Bastille Day parade. Leaders discussed the €70 billion in military aid the West has pledged Ukraine for 2026, strengthening Ukrainian air defence through an integrated missile-defence plan, and US-licensed production of Patriot interceptors in Ukraine. On the contentious question of a post-ceasefire "reassurance force," The Times reported that France and the UK together could deploy roughly 15,000 troops (about 7,500 each), well below the 30,000–60,000 some officials had floated earlier, and stationed far from the front line. The summit came as a Ukrainian strike near Stavropol in south-west Russia set an industrial zone ablaze.
story.why: With no ceasefire in sight, the meeting is Europe's attempt to show it can sustain Ukraine — and possibly police a future truce — even amid doubts about how many troops members will actually commit.

story.headline: Exceptional wildfire tears through Fontainebleau forest near Paris
story.summary: A very virulent fire burned some 800 hectares of the Fontainebleau forest southeast of Paris, forcing evacuations and partially closing the A6 on the eve of Bastille Day.
story.display_body: The blaze had burned roughly 800 hectares and was still spreading early Monday; around 15 homes were evacuated in the village of Le Vaudoué, some 400 firefighters and two water-bombing aircraft were deployed, and the fire shut part of the A6 — France's main north–south artery — at the start of the summer holiday exodus and just before the 14 July holiday. Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez said France had already lost about 17,000 hectares to fires this year, a figure he expected to reach roughly 25,000 once fully tallied — about twice the total for the same period in 2025.
story.why: A large fire in one of France's best-known forests, on the doorstep of Paris and in the middle of a European heatwave, is a vivid marker of how far the fire season has intensified this year.

fields.display_body: The blaze had burned roughly 800 hectares and was still spreading early Monday; around 15 homes were evacuated in the village of Le Vaudoué, some 400 firefighters and two water-bombing aircraft were deployed, and the fire shut part of the A6 — France's main north–south artery — at the start of the summer holiday exodus and just before the 14 July holiday. story.headline: Bangkok pub fire kills at least 27
story.summary: A fire at the Na Ladprao pub in northern Bangkok late Sunday killed at least 27 and injured 63, the deadliest such blaze in Thailand in nearly two decades.
story.display_body: The fire broke out around 11 pm and was brought under control by about 2 am Monday; 22 of the injured are in critical condition. A musician performing at the venue said he saw smoke coming from a circuit breaker near the stage before the power cut out, followed by an explosion and thick smoke that quickly filled the room; many victims were found near the restrooms at the back, and investigators are examining the ceiling materials and whether emergency exits were obstructed.
story.why: As with earlier deadly nightclub fires in the region, the toll points less to the flames than to smoke, blocked exits and flammable interiors — the recurring failures that turn a venue fire into a mass-casualty event.

fields.display_body: The fire broke out around 11 pm and was brought under control by about 2 am Monday; 22 of the injured are in critical condition. story.headline: UN debate in Geneva exposes Western reluctance to name UAE over Sudan's El-Obeid
story.summary: A 3 July emergency Human Rights Council session on imminent atrocities in besieged El-Obeid revealed Western states' hesitancy to name the UAE as backer of RSF drone strikes.
story.display_body: The rare "anticipatory" session — convened to try to prevent atrocities rather than react to them — heard warnings that El-Obeid, a strategic hub of about 400 km south-west of Khartoum in North Kordofan, could become "the next crime scene": UN monitors report escalating and apparently indiscriminate RSF drone strikes, with roughly 15 strikes in June killing at least 45 civilians and hitting hospitals, markets and homes. Reporting from Geneva describes tensions boiling over when an Emirati delegate allegedly photographed an NGO participant during informal consultations — a breach of UN rules meant to shield civil society from intimidation — prompting a British chair to call the room to order. The European Parliament has since passed a resolution explicitly naming the UAE and urging action.
story.why: The episode shows how commercial and strategic ties to Abu Dhabi are muffling accountability at the UN's main rights forum, even as monitors warn of an imminent mass-atrocity risk.

story.headline: WWF: Switzerland avoided CHF 31 billion in fossil-fuel imports over 15 years
story.summary: A new WWF study quantifies for the first time that efficiency gains and renewables spared Switzerland CHF 31bn in oil and gas imports since 2010, with transport the weak link at 5% EVs.
story.display_body: The environmental group argues that energy independence is not only a climate imperative but an economic one, since money not spent on imported fossil fuels stays in the domestic economy. The analysis flags transport as the persistent weak link, with only about 5% of the vehicle fleet electrified — meaning the sector still leaks the most money abroad and offers the largest remaining savings.
story.why: It reframes the decarbonisation debate around household and national costs rather than emissions alone — a framing that speaks directly to how energy policy shows up in Swiss bills and balance sheets.

fields.display_body: The environmental group argues that energy independence is not only a climate imperative but an economic one, since money not spent on imported fossil fuels stays in the domestic economy. story.headline: Switzerland braces for new heatwave as 2026 nears record summer
story.summary: SRF Meteo says a fresh heatwave could push 2026 past the 2003 century-summer if August heat holds, as drought grips northern Italy and a camping site closes in Goms over natural-hazard risk.
story.display_body: The heat is already biting across the region: prolonged drought in northern Italy is deepening water shortages and hurting Parmesan production, and in Switzerland's Goms valley a campsite closed over natural-hazard risk, pushing summer guests to neighbouring sites. Swiss federal heat warnings remain in force as the same system that has fed wildfires in France and Spain sits over the country.
story.why: A summer on track to challenge 2003 — Europe's benchmark deadly heat year — is a concrete signal of how routine Alpine summers are shifting, with knock-on effects on water, agriculture and tourism close to home.
